title: Compound Verbs (le/de/kar dena)
situation: Hindi loves attaching a 'completion verb' to a main verb to add nuance: politeness, finality, totality. 'le lena' (take up), 'de dena' (give over), 'kar dena' (do over) — these tiny additions transform meaning.
skills: common patterns: le lena, de dena, kar dena, ho jaana

phrases.context: 'pee lo' vs just 'pee' — adding 'lo' (from le lena) makes it sound completion-oriented and gently insistent.
'kar dungi' (kar + dena) implies 'I'll do it for you / I'll get it over with'. More committed than just 'karungi'.
'de dijiye' (de + dena polite) is more polite than just 'dijiye'. The compound softens.
'so jaana' (sleep + jaana = go) is the standard compound for 'fall asleep'. 'gaye' is past perfective.
'khaa lo' (khaa + le) = eat (it up). Without 'lo', sounds incomplete. Mothers say this constantly.
'kar do' (kar + de) = do it (over). Standard instruction-completion compound — softer than just 'karo'.

phrases.hindi: main aapka number save kar leti hoon
phrases.context: 'kar leti hoon' (kar + le, present) — 'I'll just take care of saving it'. The compound implies the action is small/quick.
phrases.pronunciation: MAIN AAP-ka NUM-ber SAVE kar LE-ti hoon

phrases.context: 'pahunch gayi' (reach + jaana) — completion via 'jaana'. Same as 'so gayi' for sleep. Native pattern: motion verbs commonly take jaana-compound for completion.

phrases.english: give me some water, won't you
phrases.context: 'pila do' (causative pilana + dena). 'do na' adds friendly insistence — the 'na' is a soft tag-question.

phrases.hindi: yeh sab samjha do bachon ko
phrases.english: explain all this to the kids
phrases.context: 'samjha do' (samjhana + dena) — explain (it over), 'do' marks the completion of the explaining for the listeners' benefit.
phrases.pronunciation: YEH sab sam-JHA do ba-CHON ko

grammar_notes: Compound verbs = main verb stem + auxiliary (le/de/kar/jaa/ho). The auxiliary loses its own meaning and just adds aspect/politeness.
'le lena' (take up): the main verb's action benefits the doer. 'khaa lo' = eat (for yourself).
'de dena' (give over): the main verb's action benefits someone else. 'de do' = hand it over (to them).
'kar dena' (do over): emphasizes completion + politeness. 'kar do' is softer/more polite than bare 'karo'.
'jaa jaana' (go): used with motion or change-of-state verbs to mark completion. 'so jaana' (fall asleep), 'pahunch jaana' (arrive).
'ho jaana' (become): change-of-state. 'theek ho gaya' (got better), 'pareshaan ho gayi' (became worried).
culture_notes: Compound verbs are EVERYWHERE in spoken Hindi. Refusing to use them makes you sound like a foreigner reading from a textbook.
Mothers and aunties use 'kha lo / pee lo / so jaao' as their core vocabulary for caring instructions. Internalize these.
The compound choice (le/de/kar/jaa) carries social information — 'le' = for you, 'de' = for me, 'kar' = polite, 'jaa' = complete.

skill_breakdown.explanation: Attached to a verb when the action benefits the doer or is self-directed. 'khaa lo' (eat — for yourself), 'pee lo' (drink up), 'samajh lo' (got it / understand for yourself).

more_examples.hindi: main ticket book kar leti hoon

skill_breakdown.explanation: Attached when the action benefits the listener or someone other than the doer. 'de do' (hand it over), 'bata do' (tell — to them), 'kar do' (do it — for them).

skill_breakdown.skill: kar dena / jaa jaana / ho jaana
skill_breakdown.explanation: 'kar dena' = do over (politeness + completion). 'jaa jaana' = go (motion / state-completion). 'ho jaana' = become (change of state). All three add a sense of 'completedness' that bare verbs lack.

more_examples.english: food is ready (lit: has become ready)

practice_prompt: You are a Hindi conversational tutor helping the user master compound verbs. Give them everyday situations (asking for water, telling a kid to sleep, requesting a favor) and have them respond using a compound verb. Correct gently when they use a bare verb where a compound is natural. Use 'aap' (the user is your student, polite default).

theory.intro: Hindi expresses many ideas through pairs of verbs working together — V1 carries the core meaning, V2 (the auxiliary) shades the WHO, HOW, and WHEN. `Khaana` is just 'to eat', but `khaa lena` is 'to eat for oneself / finish eating' and `khaa dena` is 'to eat up for someone / consume it on their behalf'. English handles this with adverbs or context; Hindi bakes it into the verb structure. Mastering compound verbs is what makes a learner's Hindi sound natural rather than translated.

sections.heading: What's a compound verb
sections.body:
A Hindi compound verb is two verbs glued into one concept. V1 (the main verb) is in stem form — no ending. V2 (the auxiliary, also called the 'vector') carries the conjugation — tense, person, gender. Together they express something neither word can on its own.

The most common auxiliaries are `lena` (take), `dena` (give), `jaana` (go), `aana` (come), `daalna` (throw/dump), and `lagna` (begin/start). Each one shades the action differently — who it's for, whether it's sudden, whether it's complete, whether it's just beginning.

examples.breakdown: bare verb — neutral, habitual or in-progress, no completion implied
khaa (V1 stem) + liya (V2 lena, past) — eating is finished, action benefits the doer

examples.english: he ate it up (it's gone / consumed it for someone)
examples.breakdown: khaa (V1 stem) + diya (V2 dena, past) — action affects/benefits someone else

callout.body: V1 is always the stem (e.g. `khaa`, not `khaana`). V2 is what gets conjugated for tense, gender, and number. Get this backwards and the sentence breaks.

sections.cutting_intro: Hi! Let's unpack one of Hindi's coolest tricks. Two verbs, glued together, become a single meaning. Watch.

quick_check.question: In a Hindi compound verb, which part carries the conjugation (tense, gender, person)?
quick_check.explanation: Right — V2 (the auxiliary, like `lena` or `dena`) is the one that gets conjugated. V1 stays in its bare stem form.

sections.heading: Lena vs dena: the for-self / for-other axis
sections.body:
The two most common auxiliaries — `lena` (take) and `dena` (give) — split actions along a single axis: who benefits?

`-lena` means the action is for the subject themselves — they take it, they get it done, they consume it. `Maine paani pee liya` = I drank water (for myself, done with it).

`-dena` means the action is for or affects someone else — given over, handed off, performed on their behalf. `Maine usse paani pila diya` = I gave them water (for them, done to/for them). This single distinction will let you sound natural in a huge fraction of everyday speech.

table.caption: Common verb + lena / dena pairs
rows: kar lena — get it done (for yourself)
kar dena — do it (for them, often a favor)
khaa dena — finish it off / consume for someone
pila dena — give a drink to / make someone drink
de dena — hand it over (to them)
bol lena — go ahead and say it
bol dena — tell it (to someone) / let it slip

callout.body: `-lena` is more common than `-dena` in everyday speech — most everyday actions are for yourself. Default to `-lena` if you're unsure; switch to `-dena` only when the listener or a third party is the beneficiary.

sections.cutting_intro: Here's the single biggest distinction: -lena means for yourself, -dena means for someone else. One axis. Memorize it and you're halfway there.

quick_check.question: You want to say 'I'll book the ticket (for myself)'. Which auxiliary do you use?
options: kar dena — `main ticket book kar dungi`
kar lena — `main ticket book kar leti hoon`
quick_check.explanation: Yes — the action is for yourself, so `-lena` is the natural pick. `kar dena` would suggest you're booking it for someone else.

sections.heading: Jaana, daalna, lagna — completion, force, beginning
sections.body:
Beyond the for-self / for-other split, Hindi has a small set of auxiliaries that shade HOW the action happens — its energy, its suddenness, its arc.

`-jaana` (go) marks completion through a transition. The action runs all the way to its endpoint. `Woh ro gayi` = she broke down crying (sudden onset that fully arrived). `Bachhe so gaye` = the kids fell asleep (the sleeping completed).

`-daalna` (throw, dump) adds force or finality, often with a slightly negative or emphatic edge. `Maine khaa daala` = I devoured it / I knocked it back. Use it when the action was forceful or impatient.

`-lagna` (begin, attach) marks the START of an action, not its completion. `Baarish hone lagi` = it started raining. `Woh rone lagi` = she started crying.

examples.breakdown: thak (V1) + gayi (jaana, fem.) — the tiredness has arrived fully
khaa (V1) + daala (daalna, past) — emphatic, forceful eating
rone (V1 oblique infinitive) + laga (lagna, past masc.) — onset, not completion

callout.body: `jaana` is the subtlest of the three — it doesn't add force or emotion, it just signals that the action reached its endpoint. If you don't know which auxiliary to use for completion, `jaana` is usually a safe default.

sections.cutting_intro: Now the energy auxiliaries — jaana for completion, daalna for force, lagna for the beginning. Each one paints the action differently.

quick_check.question: Which auxiliary marks the START of an action — like 'it started raining'?
quick_check.explanation: Right — `lagna` (literally 'attach' / 'begin') is the auxiliary for onset. `baarish hone lagi` = the rain began.

sections.heading: Kar dena vs kar lena vs kar daalna: precise differences
sections.body:
Three close-cousin constructions show up constantly with `karna` (do). Each picks a different angle on the same action — and they're not interchangeable.

`Kar dena` (do + give) = do it for someone, often as a favor or service. `Mera homework kar do` = do my homework for me. The beneficiary is the listener.

`Kar lena` (do + take) = do it for yourself, get it taken care of. `Apna homework kar lo` = do your own homework (it's on you). The beneficiary is the doer.

`Kar daalna` (do + dump) = do it forcefully, knock it out, finish it off with energy. `Yeh kaam kar daalo` = knock this work out. The energy is emphatic, almost impatient.

examples.breakdown: kar (V1) + do (dena imperative) — listener benefits the speaker
kar (V1) + lo (lena imperative) — listener is the beneficiary themselves
kar (V1) + daalo (daalna imperative) — emphatic, finish it with force

callout.body: The auxiliary you pick signals WHO benefits and with WHAT energy. Same V1, three different feelings. Native speakers switch between them without thinking — practice the contrast until it's automatic.

sections.cutting_intro: Time for the trickiest contrast. Same main verb, three different auxiliaries, three different meanings. Watch closely.

quick_check.question: Your mom asks you to do your own homework. Which compound is she most likely to use?
quick_check.explanation: Yes — `kar lo` (kar lena) says 'do it for yourself, it's on you'. `kar do` would mean she's asking you to do it for HER, which doesn't fit. `kar daalo` would be emphatic, like 'just knock it out already'.

sections.heading: Common compound patterns + how to recognize them
sections.body:
Once you know the auxiliaries, you'll start spotting compounds everywhere. Look for V1 (in stem form) followed immediately by a conjugated form of `lena`, `dena`, `jaana`, `aana`, `daalna`, or `lagna`. That's almost always a compound.

Many compounds have become so fixed that they feel like single words — `so jaana` (fall asleep), `aa jaana` (arrive), `bhool jaana` (forget). You'll learn these as units, the way English learners learn 'give up' or 'put off' as phrasal verbs.

callout.body: Compound verbs are NOT just fancier synonyms for the base verb. The auxiliary always shifts the meaning — completion, beneficiary, force, or onset. Dropping the auxiliary often produces ungrammatical Hindi, or at best a stiff, clinical sentence that sounds translated rather than spoken.

sections.cutting_intro: Last thing — let's pattern-match. Once you see the V1 + auxiliary shape, you'll spot compounds everywhere. Here are ten you'll hear constantly.

quick_check.question: You hear `main yeh kaam kal kar dunga`. What does `kar dunga` tell you?
options: The action will be done for someone else (kar + dena, future)
The action will be done for myself
quick_check.explanation: Right — `kar dunga` is `karna + dena` in the future tense, masculine. The `dena` signals the action benefits someone else. (`kar lunga` would be 'I'll do it for myself'.)

theory.wrap_up: Compound verbs are one of the things that makes spoken Hindi feel native rather than textbook. Learners often skip the auxiliary out of caution — but doing so makes you sound robotic, like you're translating word-by-word from English. Start with `lena` / `dena` (for-self vs for-other), then layer in `jaana` for completion, then `daalna` and `lagna` for emphasis and onset. The phrases below give you contrasted minimal pairs to drill the difference — pay attention to which auxiliary is doing the work in each one.
